question.text: A merchant banker is advising Company X on acquiring a controlling stake in Company Y, a listed entity. Company X proposes to pay Rs. 200 per share to the sellers plus a non-compete fee of Rs. 30 per share. The 60-day VWAP of Company Y is Rs. 190. What is the minimum open offer price per share?

answer.text: Rs. 230 per share (negotiated price plus non-compete fee)
Rs. 215 per share (average of VWAP and negotiated price plus non-compete)

explanation.text: **The Logic:** The non-compete fee of Rs. 30 per share is treated as part of the offer price under SAST Regulations. The effective price paid is Rs. 200 + Rs. 30 = Rs. 230 per share. The minimum offer price is the higher of the negotiated price (including non-compete) and the VWAP. Since Rs. 230 > Rs. 190 (VWAP), the minimum offer price is Rs. 230 per share. **The Trap:** Rs. 200 ignores the non-compete fee, which SEBI mandates must be included. This is exactly the kind of structuring SEBI prevents by treating non-compete fees as part of consideration. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series IX (Merchant Banking) tests offer price computation including all components of consideration. This is a high-priority calculation topic. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Always ask the acquirer about ALL payments being made to sellers — non-compete fees, consultancy arrangements, property deals — anything that inflates total consideration must be captured in the offer price.
